summary: Israel's defence minister announced the IDF will not withdraw from southern Lebanon and will maintain indefinite security zones in Lebanon, Syria and Gaza. The United States and Iran signed a digital agreement, but US officials say no Iranian assets have been released and sanctions remain in place. Iran plans to charge service fees for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz after a 60‑day period.
key_points: Israel's defence minister Katz stated the IDF will remain in security zones in Lebanon, Syria and Gaza without a time limit (Source: @shin_persian/7088).
Katz said the security zones will be cleared of local residents and all terror infrastructure, including houses in contact‑line villages, will be destroyed (Source: @shin_persian/7088).
Katz warned that if Iran attacks Israel because of events in Lebanon, Israel will strike Iran with full force (Source: @shin_persian/7088).
The United States and Iran signed a digital agreement on Sunday, signed by President Trump, Vice‑President Vance and Iran's parliament speaker (Source: @kianmeli1/88024).
US Vice‑President Vance told CNN that not a single dollar of Iran's frozen assets has been released and no sanctions relief has occurred (Source: @VahidOnline/76403).
Vance described the agreement as a very brief, high‑level framework of about one and a half pages, with detailed commitments to be worked out later (Source: @VahidOnline/76403).
Iranian state media Tasnim reported that after a 60‑day period Iran will charge "service fees" to vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz (Source: @shin_persian/7096).
Tasnim also noted the agreement includes a clause guaranteeing Lebanon's sovereignty and territorial integrity (Source: @shin_persian/7096).

sections.executive_summary: Israel's defence minister announced the IDF will not withdraw from southern Lebanon and will keep security zones in Lebanon, Syria and Gaza indefinitely. The United States and Iran have signed a digital agreement, yet US officials confirm no Iranian assets have been unfrozen and sanctions remain. Iran plans to impose service fees on ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz after a 60‑day window.

points: US Vice‑President Vance told CNN that not a single dollar of Iran's frozen assets has been released and no sanctions relief has been granted (Source: @VahidOnline/76403).
Vance described the US‑Iran memorandum as a brief, high‑level framework of roughly one and a half pages, with detailed commitments to be negotiated later (Source: @VahidOnline/76403).

Iranian state media Tasnim reported that after a 60‑day period Iran will charge "service fees" to vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz, as stipulated in the agreement (Source: @shin_persian/7096).
The same Tasnim report said the agreement's first article includes a phrase guaranteeing Lebanon's sovereignty and respect for its territorial integrity (Source: @shin_persian/7096).

US officials indicated that the agreement does not yet provide any concrete benefits to Iran until it fulfills its commitments, including halting support for terrorist groups (Source: @VahidOnline/76403).
Defence Minister Yoav Katz said the IDF will remain in the security zones in Lebanon, Syria and Gaza without any time limit to protect Israel's borders and communities (Source: @shin_persian/7088).

Katz announced that the security zones will be cleared of local residents and all terror infrastructure, both above and below ground, including houses in contact‑line villages, will be destroyed (Source: @shin_persian/7088).

He described holding the security zones as one of the IDF's greatest achievements in the war and stated Israel opposes any withdrawal from Lebanon despite existing pressures (Source: @shin_persian/7088).
Katz warned that if Iran attacks Israel because of events in Lebanon, Israel will strike Iran with full force (Source: @shin_persian/7088).
An Israeli official told i24NEWS that Israel's ability to act against Hezbollah's growth is not contingent on a withdrawal from Lebanon (Source: @inon_yttach/9006).
Israel's defence minister reaffirmed that the IDF will not withdraw from southern Lebanon and will keep the area under security control (Source: @shin_persian/7088).
He stated that the security zones will be cleared of local residents and all terror infrastructure will be destroyed, including houses used as terror outposts (Source: @shin_persian/7088).
He warned of a full‑scale Israeli response if Iran attacks Israel due to events in Lebanon (Source: @shin_persian/7088).

key_developments: Israel's defence minister announced the IDF will remain indefinitely in security zones across Lebanon, Syria and Gaza, clearing residents and destroying terror infrastructure (Source: @shin_persian/7088).
The United States and Iran signed a digital agreement on Sunday, electronically endorsed by President Trump, Vice‑President Vance and Iran's parliament speaker (Source: @kianmeli1/88024).
US Vice‑President Vance confirmed that no Iranian assets have been unfrozen and no sanctions relief has been granted despite the agreement (Source: @VahidOnline/76403).
Vance described the US‑Iran memorandum as a brief, high‑level framework of about one and a half pages, with detailed terms to be negotiated later (Source: @VahidOnline/76403).
Iranian state media Tasnim reported that after a 60‑day period Iran will impose service fees on vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz (Source: @shin_persian/7096).
The same Tasnim report noted the agreement includes a clause guaranteeing Lebanon's sovereignty and territorial integrity (Source: @shin_persian/7096).
An Israeli official told i24NEWS that Israel can still defend itself against Hezbollah without a withdrawal from Lebanon (Source: @inon_yttach/9006).
sections.threat_assessment: The continuation of Israeli IDF presence in southern Lebanon, Syria and Gaza sustains a high‑risk environment for cross‑border attacks and may provoke retaliatory strikes from Hezbollah or Iran. The US‑Iran digital agreement, while politically significant, has not yet translated into sanctions relief, leaving Iranian economic pressure intact and potentially fueling regional tensions. Iran's planned service fees in the Strait of Hormuz could affect commercial shipping and raise the risk of naval confrontations, especially if perceived as punitive by regional actors.
sections.regional_response: Israel is reinforcing its security posture, emphasizing deterrence against Hezbollah and warning of a strong response to any Iranian aggression. The United States maintains a cautious stance, emphasizing that any benefits to Iran are contingent on full compliance with the agreement's commitments, particularly the cessation of support for terrorist groups. Regional Gulf states are likely monitoring the Strait of Hormuz fee proposal closely, as it may impact their energy export routes and maritime security calculations.
sections.intelligence_notes: US intelligence agencies have expressed doubts about Iran's willingness to meet its nuclear and anti‑terrorism commitments, suggesting possible delays in implementation of the agreement. Israeli statements indicate a policy of indefinite occupation of security zones, which could complicate diplomatic efforts to de‑escalate the Lebanon front. Monitoring of maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz will be critical to assess Iran's enforcement of service fees and any escalation in naval incidents.
